facts.text: New Brunswick was named in 1724 after King George I, the Duke of Brunswick, linking the city to the English royal house.
Rutgers University, founded in 1766 in New Brunswick, was the eighth college in the colonies and the only state university to predate the Revolutionary War.
The first recorded European inhabitant of the area was Daniel Cooper, who lived near the present Albany Street bridge in the late 1600s.
In 1730, Joseph Borden established a transportation system linking New Brunswick with New York City and Philadelphia, boosting early commerce.
The Johnson brothers launched the first pharmaceutical company in New Brunswick in 1886, setting the stage for the city’s later reputation as a health‑care hub.
Crosswicks Creek Site III, an American Revolutionary War era location in New Brunswick, was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1990 for its military significance.
